So the witnesses make the verse the inheritance by number: The witnesses note the unto these: to the numbered, and none else. The witnesses note the the land divided: parted among the tribes. The expositors observe the unto these: to the numbered, and none else. The expositors observe the the land divided: parted among the tribes. The expositors observe the according to the names: each portion measured by the count. The expositors observe the certain and fair: promised to the numbered, parted by their number. 54:
"To many thou shalt give the more inheritance, and to few thou shalt give the less inheritance: to every one shall his inheritance be given according to those that were numbered of him." The witnesses note the rule of equity — the larger tribe a larger portion, the smaller a smaller, each according to its number. The witnesses note that Gill notes a more numerous tribe was to have a greater part of the land, a smaller tribe a lesser share; the direction is given to Moses but not for him personally, for he never entered the land. The witnesses note that each was to receive according to those numbered of it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to many, more — the larger tribe a larger portion; the to few, less — the smaller tribe a smaller share; the according to the number — each measured by its muster; the a just hand — the fairness of God in dividing his gifts.

The witnesses agree the rule was one of proportion and justice: the size of each tribe's inheritance answered to the size of its number, the many receiving more and the few less, that none be crowded nor any waste with too much; the equity of the law — every one served according to his need and number — shows the fairness of God in the dividing of his gifts, the inheritance measured out with a just hand to each.

So the witnesses make the verse the rule of equity: The witnesses note the to many, more: the larger tribe a larger portion. The witnesses note the to few, less: the smaller tribe a smaller share. The witnesses note the according to the number: each measured by its muster. The witnesses note the a just hand: the fairness of God in dividing his gifts. The expositors observe the to many, more: the larger tribe a larger portion. The expositors observe the to few, less: the smaller tribe a smaller share. 55:
"Notwithstanding the land shall be divided by lot: according to the names of the tribes of their fathers they shall inherit." The witnesses note the second rule joined to the first — the land also divided by lot, that the parting be of God. The witnesses note that Gill notes the division was by lot, that it might appear to be according to the will of God, and not left to the judgment of the chief magistrate, though bound by the rule of proportion. The witnesses note that Clarke discusses the word for "lot," the stone or pebble used in casting it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the by lot — which portion to which tribe settled by lot; the the will of God — that the parting be his, not man's; the joined to number — equity and sovereignty wed together; the the LORD disposing — the lot of the LORD (Prov. 16:33).

The witnesses agree the joining of number and lot weds equity to sovereignty: the size of each portion was fixed by the tribe's number, but which portion fell to which tribe was settled by lot, that the allotment might be seen as God's own choice and not man's favour; the rule guards against partiality and quiets all dispute, for "the lot is cast into the lap, but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Prov. 16:33) — the inheritance both fairly measured and divinely assigned.

So the witnesses make the verse divided by lot: The witnesses note the by lot: which portion to which tribe settled by lot. The witnesses note the the will of God: that the parting be his, not man's. The witnesses note the joined to number: equity and sovereignty wed together. The witnesses note the the LORD disposing: the lot of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The expositors observe the by lot: which portion to which tribe settled by lot. The expositors observe the the will of God: that the parting be his, not man's. The expositors observe the joined to number: equity and sovereignty wed together. The expositors observe the the LORD disposing: the lot of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The believer is taught to mark the by lot, the the will of God, the joined to number, the the LORD disposing, and to trust the God who orders his people's portion. The verse thus records that the land was also divided by lot — the allotment God's own choice, equity and sovereignty wed, the lot disposed by the LORD (Prov. 16:33).
56:
"According to the lot shall the possession thereof be divided between many and few." The witnesses note the two rules held together — the possession divided by lot, yet between many and few according to number. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was by the determination of the lot that the land was divided by inheritance, and that was of the Lord. The witnesses note that Wesley reads it — the share that fell by lot to each tribe was distributed to its families and persons in such proportions as their numbers required. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the according to the lot — the place decided by lot; the between many and few — the size by the number; the within each tribe — the families served by their proportions; the no room for envy — divine choice and equity together.

The witnesses agree the verse binds the lot and the number into one harmonious rule: the lot decided the place, the number decided the size, and within each tribe's lot the families received their shares by their proportions; the wisdom of the arrangement — divine choice and human equity working together — left no room for envy or wrong, the whole land parted so that each tribe and family had exactly what God assigned and their number warranted.

So the witnesses make the verse lot and number together: The witnesses note the according to the lot: the place decided by lot. The witnesses note the between many and few: the size by the number. The witnesses note the within each tribe: the families served by their proportions. The witnesses note the no room for envy: divine choice and equity together. The expositors observe the according to the lot: the place decided by lot. The expositors observe the between many and few: the size by the number. The expositors observe the within each tribe: the families served by their proportions. The expositors observe the no room for envy: divine choice and equity together. The believer is taught to mark the according to the lot, the between many and few, the within each tribe, the no room for envy, and to trust the God who orders his people's portion. The verse thus records that the possession was divided by lot, yet between many and few by number — divine choice and human equity working together, leaving no room for envy or wrong.
57:
"And these are they that were numbered of the Levites after their families: of Gershon, the family of the Gershonites..." The witnesses note the Levites numbered apart from the rest, by their three families of Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Levites were numbered not with the rest of the tribes but by themselves, as at the first; the three principal families were the Gershonite, Kohathite, and Merarite, from the three sons of Levi. The witnesses note that not all the sons are named, only the chief families. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the numbered by themselves — not with the rest of the tribes; the three families — gershon, Kohath, and Merari; the the chief houses — reckoned by the sons of Levi; the a distinct calling — set apart to the sanctuary, not the sword.

The witnesses agree the separate numbering of Levi marks their distinct calling: they were not mustered with the armies of Israel, for theirs was not the warfare of the sword but the service of the sanctuary, and they had no share in the division of the land; the counting of Levi apart, by its three ancient families, keeps before us that this tribe stood between the people and the holy things, set apart wholly to the LORD and reckoned by another rule than the rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the Levites apart: The witnesses note the numbered by themselves: not with the rest of the tribes. The witnesses note the three families: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. The witnesses note the the chief houses: reckoned by the sons of Levi. The witnesses note the a distinct calling: set apart to the sanctuary, not the sword. The expositors observe the numbered by themselves: not with the rest of the tribes. The expositors observe the three families: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. The expositors observe the the chief houses: reckoned by the sons of Levi. The expositors observe the a distinct calling: set apart to the sanctuary, not the sword. The believer is taught to mark the numbered by themselves, the three families, the the chief houses, the a distinct calling, and to trust the God who orders his people's portion. The verse thus records that the Levites were numbered apart by their three families — their distinct calling marked, set apart to the sanctuary and reckoned by another rule than the rest.
58:
"These are the families of the Levites... the family of the Korathites. And Kohath begat Amram." The witnesses note the Levite families, and the line of Kohath through Amram — the family of Moses and Aaron. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch list the Levite families — Libnites, Hebronites, Mahlites, Mushites, Korathites — and that Kohath begat Amram. The witnesses note that the line of Amram is the line of the priesthood and the leaders. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Levite families — libnites, Hebronites, and the rest; the the Korathites — the spared line of Korah among them; the Kohath begat Amram — the line of the leaders and priests; the the heart of the history — moses, Aaron, and Miriam from this house.

The witnesses agree the genealogy turns at Amram to the very heart of Israel's history: from the family of Kohath, through Amram, came Moses and Aaron and Miriam, the leaders and the priesthood of the nation; the bare naming of "Kohath begat Amram" carries the whole weight of the deliverance and the law, the house of Levi honoured above all in giving Israel its mediator and its high priest — and even the line of Korah, the Korathites, preserved among the families, the children spared standing in the record.

So the witnesses make the verse the line of Kohath: The witnesses note the the Levite families: Libnites, Hebronites, and the rest. The witnesses note the the Korathites: the spared line of Korah among them. The witnesses note the Kohath begat Amram: the line of the leaders and priests. The witnesses note the the heart of the history: Moses, Aaron, and Miriam from this house. The expositors observe the the Levite families: Libnites, Hebronites, and the rest. The expositors observe the the Korathites: the spared line of Korah among them. The expositors observe the Kohath begat Amram: the line of the leaders and priests. 59:
"And the name of Amram's wife was Jochebed, the daughter of Levi, whom her mother bare to Levi in Egypt: and she bare unto Amram Aaron and Moses, and Miriam their sister." The witnesses note Jochebed, daughter of Levi, the mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam. Heb. 11:23).

So the witnesses make the verse the mother of the deliverers: The witnesses note the Jochebed: the daughter of Levi, Amram's wife. The witnesses note the born in Egypt: of the tribe of Levi there. The witnesses note the Aaron, Moses, Miriam: the three she bore. The witnesses note the honoured in the record: the mother of faith remembered (Heb. 11:23). 61:

The witnesses agree the census preserves, even here, the memory of judgment: the death of Aaron's two eldest sons for their presumption is recalled in the very genealogy of the priestly house, a warning written into the record that even the sons of the high priest were not spared when they offered what God commanded not (Lev. 10:1-2); the line of the priesthood passed not through them but through Eleazar and Ithamar, the fire that consumed the presumptuous standing as a perpetual caution to all who draw near to God.

So the witnesses make the verse Nadab and Abihu: The witnesses note the died: for offering strange fire. The witnesses note the before the LORD: presuming in the holy service. The witnesses note the the warning recalled: even the priest's sons not spared (Lev. 10:1-2). The witnesses note the the line passing on: through Eleazar and Ithamar. The expositors observe the died: for offering strange fire. The expositors observe the before the LORD: presuming in the holy service. The expositors observe the the warning recalled: even the priest's sons not spared (Lev. 10:1-2). The expositors observe the the line passing on: through Eleazar and Ithamar. So the witnesses make the verse the Levite portion: The witnesses note the 23,000: the Levites from a month old. The witnesses note the not from twenty: counted as the LORD's, not as soldiers. The witnesses note the no inheritance: no share of the land given them. The witnesses note the the LORD their portion: their inheritance God himself (Num. 18:20). The witnesses agree the closing note fixes the place and the men: the census was taken by Moses and Eleazar — the lawgiver and the high priest — in the plains of Moab, with Jordan and Jericho in view, the people poised at the very threshold of the promised land; the naming of the place is itself a word of hope, the long wandering ended and the inheritance in sight, the numbered host ready to cross over into the rest so long delayed.

So the witnesses make the verse the place of the muster: The witnesses note the Moses and Eleazar: the lawgiver and the high priest. The witnesses note the in the plains of Moab: by Jordan near Jericho. The witnesses note the on the edge of the land: the people at the threshold. The witnesses note the a word of hope: the wandering ended, the inheritance in sight. The expositors observe the Moses and Eleazar: the lawgiver and the high priest. The expositors observe the in the plains of Moab: by Jordan near Jericho. The expositors observe the on the edge of the land: the people at the threshold. The expositors observe the a word of hope: the wandering ended, the inheritance in sight. 64:
"But among these there was not a man of them whom Moses and Aaron the priest numbered, when they numbered the children of Israel in the wilderness of Sinai." The witnesses note that not one of the first muster, taken at Sinai, remained among this second census. The witnesses note that Gill notes this respects the numbering of the Israelites, not the Levites, for some of Levi numbered then were still living, as Eleazar. The witnesses note that of the lay tribes not a man of the first census survived to the second. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not a man — of those numbered at Sinai; the the first muster — thirty-eight years before; the the whole generation dead — fallen in the wilderness; the the threatening fulfilled — they entered not for unbelief (Heb. 3:19).

The witnesses agree the contrast of the two musters is the most solemn note of the chapter: of all the multitude numbered at Sinai thirty-eight years before, not one fighting man was left in this new census — the whole generation had died in the wilderness, as God had sworn; the census thus seals the truth of the divine threatening, the carcasses fallen to a man, a warning the apostle takes up, that they could not enter in because of unbelief (Heb. 3:19).

So the witnesses make the verse none of the first remained: The witnesses note the not a man: of those numbered at Sinai. The witnesses note the the first muster: thirty-eight years before. The witnesses note the the whole generation dead: fallen in the wilderness. The witnesses note the the threatening fulfilled: they entered not for unbelief (Heb. 3:19). The expositors observe the not a man: of those numbered at Sinai. The expositors observe the the first muster: thirty-eight years before. The expositors observe the the whole generation dead: fallen in the wilderness. The expositors observe the the threatening fulfilled: they entered not for unbelief (Heb. 3:19). 65:
"For the LORD had said of them, They shall surely die in the wilderness. And there was not left a man of them, save Caleb the son of Jephunneh, and Joshua the son of Nun." The witnesses note the closing seal — the LORD's word fulfilled, none left save Caleb and Joshua. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was threatened of them and now fulfilled; there was not left a man save Caleb and Joshua, the only two of the spies who brought a good report. The witnesses note that all the rest, and those who murmured at the ill report, were now dead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the they shall surely die — the LORD's threat on the generation; the not left a man — the whole fallen in the wilderness; the save Caleb and Joshua — the two faithful spies preserved; the double faithfulness — god true in judgment and in mercy.

The witnesses agree the chapter ends on the double faithfulness of God — in judgment and in mercy: his threat that the rebellious generation should die in the wilderness was fulfilled to the last man, yet his promise to spare the two faithful spies was equally kept, Caleb and Joshua alone surviving to enter the land; the closing words are at once a warning against unbelief and a comfort to faith, the God who is true to his threatenings no less true to his promises, the faithful preserved where the unbelieving fell.

So the witnesses make the verse the word fulfilled: The witnesses note the they shall surely die: the LORD's threat on the generation. The witnesses note the not left a man: the whole fallen in the wilderness. The witnesses note the save Caleb and Joshua: the two faithful spies preserved. The witnesses note the double faithfulness: God true in judgment and in mercy. The expositors observe the they shall surely die: the LORD's threat on the generation.
